text:
PET/CT SCAN WITH FDG - WHOLE BODY

CLINICAL INDICATION: 62-year-old female with newly identified 4.8 cm spiculated right lower lobe mass and ipsilateral mediastinal/subcarinal lymphadenopathy on prior CT chest, presenting with exertional dyspnea and unintentional weight loss. Referred for metabolic staging prior to tissue sampling and multidisciplinary treatment planning.

TECHNIQUE: Whole-body FDG PET/CT performed from skull base to mid-thigh following intravenous administration of 11.4 mCi of 18F-fluorodeoxyglucose. Blood glucose prior to injection: 94 mg/dL. Uptake period: 62 minutes. Low-dose CT performed for attenuation correction and anatomic localization. Patient fasted for 6 hours prior to injection. No intravenous contrast administered for CT component. Images reviewed in axial, coronal, and sagittal planes. Brain not included in this study; separate brain MRI has been requested for complete staging workup.

---

FINDINGS:

THORAX:

Right Lower Lobe Primary Mass: There is a dominant, intensely hypermetabolic mass in the posterior segment of the right lower lobe measuring approximately 4.8 cm in greatest axial dimension. The lesion demonstrates markedly elevated FDG uptake with an SUVmax of 14.2. The mass has a spiculated morphology on the low-dose CT component with associated pleural tethering, consistent with prior diagnostic CT characterization. There is no associated pleural effusion. The mass abuts but does not clearly invade the right hemidiaphragm.

Mediastinal and Hilar Lymph Nodes:
- Station 4R (right lower paratracheal): Enlarged and hypermetabolic lymph node cluster measuring approximately 1.8 cm in short axis, with an SUVmax of 9.8. This represents the most prominent mediastinal nodal station involved.
- Station 7 (subcarinal): Hypermetabolic subcarinal lymph node(s) with SUVmax of 11.3. Enlarged on CT with short axis approximately 1.6 cm, demonstrating confluent metabolic activity. Notably, the degree of FDG avidity at this station is the highest among all nodal regions.
- Station 10R (right hilar): Mildly enlarged right hilar lymph nodes with borderline FDG uptake (SUVmax approximately 3.1), may represent reactive adenopathy versus low-level involvement; interpretation is equivocal at this station given proximity to the primary mass and background mediastinal blood pool activity.
- Contralateral (left-sided) mediastinal and hilar nodal stations: No pathologically enlarged or FDG-avid lymph nodes identified. Stations 4L, 5, 6, 10L without abnormal metabolic activity.
- Supraclavicular and axillary regions: No abnormal FDG avidity in either supraclavicular fossa or axillary basins.

Lungs (otherwise): No additional FDG-avid pulmonary nodules or masses. Small scattered low-density foci in the right lung base likely represent atelectasis; no metabolic activity. The left lung is clear without nodularity or consolidation.

Pleura: No pleural effusion. No pleural nodularity or abnormal pleural FDG uptake.

Cardiovascular: Heart and great vessels demonstrate physiologic FDG distribution. No pericardial effusion. No focal myocardial abnormality. Mild coronary arterial calcifications noted on CT, expected for age.

---

ABDOMEN AND PELVIS:

Liver: No focal hepatic lesions with abnormal FDG avidity. Mild homogeneous background hepatic FDG uptake within normal limits. No hepatic masses or focal areas of increased uptake to suggest metastatic involvement. Liver morphology appears normal on CT.

Spleen: Normal size and homogeneous FDG uptake. No splenic lesions.

Adrenal Glands: Bilateral adrenal glands are normal in size and demonstrate physiologic FDG uptake. No nodularity. No metabolic abnormality to suggest adrenal involvement.

Kidneys/Ureters/Bladder: Physiologic FDG excretion in bilateral collecting systems and bladder. No renal masses with abnormal metabolic activity. Incidental note of small bilateral renal cortical hypodensities on CT, too small to characterize, likely simple cysts.

Pancreas: No focal FDG-avid lesions. Normal morphology.

Gastrointestinal Tract: Physiologic bowel FDG activity throughout. No focal areas of abnormal bowel uptake.

Retroperitoneum: No pathologically enlarged or FDG-avid para-aortic, aortocaval, or iliac lymph nodes.

Pelvis: No pelvic lymphadenopathy. No FDG-avid pelvic masses.

---

MUSCULOSKELETAL:

Axial and Appendicular Skeleton (imaged portion): No focal areas of abnormal FDG uptake in the visualized osseous structures to suggest skeletal metastases. Vertebral body heights maintained. No lytic or sclerotic lesions identified on CT.

Soft tissues: No focal FDG-avid soft tissue masses.

---

NECK (imaged portions, skull base to thoracic inlet):

No FDG-avid cervical lymphadenopathy. Thyroid gland: mild heterogeneous uptake with no dominant hypermetabolic nodule; this is a non-specific finding, and clinical correlation with thyroid function tests is recommended if not already performed (patient has known hypothyroidism on levothyroxine).

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Intensely hypermetabolic right lower lobe mass (SUVmax 14.2), markedly FDG-avid and morphologically spiculated -- highly suspicious for primary lung malignancy as previously described. No change in size from the recent diagnostic CT.

2. Hypermetabolic ipsilateral mediastinal lymphadenopathy involving the right paratracheal (station 4R, SUVmax 9.8) and subcarinal (station 7, SUVmax 11.3) stations, consistent with nodal involvement by malignancy. Borderline FDG avidity in the right hilar nodes (station 10R) is less certain.

3. No FDG-avid distant metastatic disease identified in the abdomen, pelvis, or osseous structures surveyed. Adrenal glands are metabolically normal.

4. No contralateral mediastinal or supraclavicular nodal FDG avidity.

5. Brain was not included in this study. Given the clinical suspicion for lung malignancy with potential for CNS metastases, MRI brain with and without contrast is strongly recommended to complete staging evaluation.

Overall, the metabolic pattern is most consistent with locoregionally advanced primary lung malignancy with ipsilateral mediastinal nodal disease. Correlation with tissue diagnosis and dedicated brain imaging is recommended before finalizing staging and treatment planning.
